originalTitle: South African leader warns anti-migrant protesters ahead of unofficial deadline
title: Friends and Neighbours: What's Happening in South Africa?
content:
Hello from Sunny South Africa!

Hello everyone! It's your friendly reporter here, bringing you news from our amazing continent, Africa. Today, we're going to talk about something happening in a beautiful country called South Africa. It's a land of big mountains, vast deserts, and friendly people. South Africa is at the very bottom tip of Africa, like a colourful shoe on a big foot! It's a wonderful place with lots of sunshine and many different animals, like lions and elephants. We all love to hear good news from our African family, and sometimes we need to talk about things that are a little tricky.

Who are Our Neighbours?

Imagine your school. You have friends from different classes, right? Sometimes, people from one town move to another town to live. In Africa, people sometimes move from one country to another country. These people are our neighbours, or sometimes we call them 'migrants' if they move to a new country. They come from countries like Zimbabwe, Mozambique, Nigeria, and many more. They are all from our big African family. They come to South Africa to find jobs, or to be safe, or to join their families who are already there.

Why Do People Move?

People move for many reasons. Sometimes, their home country might not have enough jobs. So, they travel to a place like South Africa where they hope to find work. Finding a job helps them earn money to send home to their families. This money helps their brothers, sisters, and parents buy food, clothes, and go to school. It's a way for them to help their loved ones have a better life. Sometimes, people move because they want to learn new things. Maybe there's a special school or a new skill they want to learn in a different country. It's like going on an adventure to get smarter! **Did you know?** Africa has 54 countries! That's a lot of different flags and languages, but we are all part of one big family!

A Little Problem

Now, sometimes, when many people move to a new place, some of the people who already live there might get worried. They might worry there won't be enough jobs for everyone. Or they might worry about sharing things. Some groups of people in South Africa have been telling people from other African countries to leave. They have even given a day, like a 'deadline', for them to go. This is like telling someone from a different class that they can't play in your playground. These groups are called 'anti-migrant groups'. 'Anti' means against. So, they are against people who have moved from other countries. They have been having protests, which is when people gather to show they are unhappy about something. **Did you know?** The word 'protest' comes from an old word that means to say something clearly!

What Did Our Leader Say?

The main leader of South Africa is like the head of a very big family. He is called President Cyril Ramaphosa. He heard about these worries and these groups telling people to leave. President Ramaphosa spoke up. He said that this is not right. He said that everyone in South Africa should be treated with kindness and respect. He said that people from other African countries are our brothers and sisters. He wants everyone to live together peacefully. He also reminded everyone that South Africa is a country that welcomes all Africans. He wants South Africa to be a home for everyone who needs one, and for everyone to work together to build a better future. **Did you know?** A President is like the captain of a very big ship – a country! They help make sure everyone is safe and happy.

Africa is One Big Family

Think about your own family. You might have cousins, aunts, and uncles. They are all part of your family, even if they live in different houses or different towns. Africa is like that too! We are all connected. When people move from one African country to another, they are still our neighbours, still our family. They bring new ideas, new foods, and new ways of doing things that can make everyone stronger. Imagine a big puzzle. Each country is a puzzle piece. When all the pieces fit together, the picture is beautiful and strong. That's how Africa is!

Helping Each Other Grow

When people from different places work together, amazing things can happen. A person from Zimbabwe might be a great builder, helping to build new schools in South Africa. A person from South Africa might share new farming ideas with a farmer from Mozambique. We learn from each other. We help each other. This makes our whole continent stronger and better for everyone. It's like sharing your toys with a friend – everyone has more fun! **Did you know?** Many famous African leaders, like Nelson Mandela from South Africa, taught us to be kind and to treat everyone equally!

Being Kind is Important

It's very important to always be kind to everyone, no matter where they come from. We should treat others the way we want to be treated. Our parents and teachers always tell us this, right? When we are kind and welcoming, we make our communities happier and safer places for everyone. It makes our hearts feel warm too! So, the President wants everyone to remember to be kind. He wants everyone to feel safe and welcome, and to not be afraid to be in South Africa. **Did you know?** The word 'Ubuntu' from South Africa means 'I am because we are'. It's a beautiful way to say we are all connected and need each other!

Looking Forward Together

So, even though some people were worried, our President is working hard to make sure everyone is safe and happy in South Africa. He wants to remind everyone that we are stronger when we are together. We hope that everyone can live together peacefully and kindly. Let's all remember to be good friends and neighbours to everyone, everywhere in Africa. Keep smiling, and keep being kind!
summary: In South Africa, some people from other African countries are leaving because certain groups told them to go, but the country's leader, President Ramaphosa, says everyone should be kind and live together peacefully.
whyMatters: This is important because everyone in Africa should feel safe and welcome in any country. Being kind to our neighbours and working together makes all of Africa stronger and happier for everyone.

originalTitle: Burnham pledges devolution and discipline if he becomes UK prime minister
title: Meet Andy and His Big Ideas for the UK!
content:
Jambo, my amazing friends! It's your friend, Reporter Kiki, reporting live from sunny Africa! Today, we're going on an exciting adventure to a country far, far away called the United Kingdom. It's a place with cool red buses and old castles, just like some of our amazing places here in Africa! We're going to learn about a grown-up named Andy. He has some really big ideas for his country, and he wants a very, very important job!

What is the United Kingdom?

First, let's learn a little about the United Kingdom. Imagine our big continent of Africa, so huge and full of different countries, right? The United Kingdom is a country, just like Kenya or Ghana, but it's on a big island far away across the sea. It has many cities, like London with its big clock tower, and bustling towns. Many, many people live there, and they all need good leaders to help everyone live happily, safely, and fairly. Just like in our own villages and towns, leaders are important for guiding everyone.

Who is Andy and What Does He Want?

Now, back to our friend Andy! He is a grown-up who works very hard to help people in his country. He wants to be like the 'head teacher' of the whole country! In the United Kingdom, they call this super important job the 'Prime Minister'. The Prime Minister is like the main boss who helps make big decisions for everyone in the whole country. It's a very big responsibility! Andy hopes to be the next Prime Minister and has told everyone about his big plans.

Andy's Big Idea: Devolution!

One of Andy's biggest ideas is something a bit tricky to say, but it's really, really cool! It's called 'devolution'. Can you try to say that with me? De-vo-lu-tion! Good job! What does 'devolution' mean? Imagine our village or town. Right now, maybe one big leader in the capital city makes all the rules for everyone, even for your small village. Andy says, 'No, no! Let's let the people in different towns and cities make more of their own rules and decisions!' It's like this: Imagine our school. The head teacher makes many rules for the whole school, right? But what if the head teacher said, 'Okay, Class 2, you can decide what story we read this week!' And 'Class 3, you can decide what game to play outside!' That would be devolution! It means giving power from the big boss (the Prime Minister) to smaller bosses in different local places. Andy thinks this will make people in different towns feel more important and help their towns solve their own problems better. They know what's best for them! Did you know? Many countries around the world, even some in Africa, try to share power like this! It helps different parts of the country feel heard and special, so they don't feel forgotten by the big capital city.

Andy's Other Big Idea: Discipline!

Andy also talks a lot about 'discipline'. This means making sure everyone does their job well and follows the rules. It's like when our teacher tells us to line up nicely for lunch, or finish our homework on time before we go to play. That's discipline! Andy wants to make sure that the grown-ups who work in the government – the people who help the Prime Minister – always do their very best job and follow all the rules to help the country. He wants things to be fair and well-organized for everyone. If everyone does their job properly and follows the rules, then the country will run smoothly, like a well-oiled bicycle that is easy to ride! This means no one is lazy, and everyone works hard to help all the people in the UK. Did you know? The word 'discipline' comes from an old word that means 'to teach'. So, discipline is also about teaching people the right way to do things and helping them to be their best!

The Little Problem: Not Answering Questions

Now, here's a little tricky part of the story. Andy talked about all his great ideas. He told everyone what he wanted to do. But then, after he finished talking, some other grown-ups wanted to ask him questions. Like when your teacher finishes explaining something, and you raise your hand to ask, 'Teacher, can we play football instead of netball today?' But Andy didn't take many questions. This made some people feel a little bit sad or confused. They thought, 'Hmm, why didn't he want to answer our questions? We want to know more!' It's important for leaders to listen and answer questions so everyone understands and trusts them. It's like if your friend tells you a fun story but then won't let you ask about the exciting ending! You'd feel a bit puzzled, wouldn't you?

Why is it important to ask questions?

Asking questions is how we learn more and how we make sure we understand things! For a leader, answering questions shows they are brave, honest, and ready to listen to everyone. It helps people know that the leader is thinking about everyone's ideas, not just their own. So, when Andy didn't answer many questions, people worried a little if he would always listen to them if he got the big job. A good leader always listens to the people they lead. Did you know? In many countries, leaders have special meetings called 'press conferences' where reporters ask them questions all the time! It's a very important part of their job to explain things and listen to feedback.

What Happens Next?

So, there you have it, my amazing friends! Andy has big dreams for the United Kingdom. He wants to share power with towns and make sure everyone does their job well. And like all leaders, he's learning that listening and answering questions is just as important as having great ideas! It's exciting to see how leaders try to make their countries better, even far away from us in Africa. Maybe one day, YOU will have big ideas to help YOUR community and YOUR country too! Keep learning, keep asking questions, and keep shining bright! Goodbye for now from your African reporter Kiki!
summary: A man named Andy in the United Kingdom wants a very important job and has big ideas to share power with different towns and make sure everyone works well, but some people wished he had answered more questions about his plans.
whyMatters: This matters because choosing a good leader with good ideas helps a country be happy and fair, and it's important for leaders to listen and answer questions so everyone trusts them.
